choices.rationale: Evaporative loss from the prep is transient and self-limited once the solution dries, so although it contributes it is not the dominant first-hour mechanism.

choices.text: Convection from cool OR airflow moving across the infant, accounting for the great majority of loss
choices.rationale: Convection from laminar OR airflow is significant but, in a still infant under drapes, is generally a smaller fraction than radiant loss and is partly mitigated by reducing air movement.

choices.text: Radiation from the warm skin and exposed viscera to cooler surrounding surfaces, accounting for roughly 40% of loss and best countered by raising ambient/radiant temperature and covering exposed surfaces
choices.rationale: Correct: radiation to cooler surrounding surfaces is the single largest contributor to perioperative heat loss (about 40%), magnified in neonates by their high surface-area-to-mass ratio, thin skin, and lack of insulating fat, and is best mitigated by raising the room temperature, using radiant warmers/forced-air, and covering exposed skin and bowel.

choices.text: Respiratory loss through the breathing circuit, since neonates have high minute ventilation relative to body mass
choices.rationale: Respiratory heat and water loss accounts for only roughly 10% of total loss and is further reduced by heated, humidified circuit gases, so it is not the leading target.

explanation: Perioperative heat loss occurs by radiation (~40%, the largest fraction), convection (~30%), evaporation (~20%), and conduction (~10%), with respiratory loss comprising a small additional component. Neonates are exquisitely vulnerable because their large surface-area-to-mass ratio, minimal subcutaneous fat, thin skin, and inability to shiver are compounded by an exposed body and open viscera. Because radiation dominates, the highest-yield interventions are raising the ambient room temperature, using radiant/forced-air warmers, and covering all exposed surfaces; passive measures alone are inadequate for a neonate this size.
subtopic: Neonatal thermoregulation — relative contribution of heat-loss mechanisms and intraoperative warming strategy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (radiation, ~40%) is the single best answer and standard board teaching — radiation is the largest single contributor to perioperative heat loss, magnified in neonates by high surface-area-to-mass ratio, thin skin, and minimal fat. Notably the ~40% figure maps precisely to the classic NEONATAL thermoneutral breakdown (radiation 39%, convection 34%, evaporation 24%, conduction 3%), which is the correct frame for this neonatal stem rather than the adult-OR ~60% figure; the explanation's breakdown (radiation ~40/convection ~30/evaporation ~20/conduction ~10) is an acceptable textbook approximation. Distractors are genuinely inferior: conduction is mitigated/minor, prep evaporation is transient and self-limited, convection is real but second to radiation, and respiratory loss is ~10% and reducible. One mild adversarial tension: with open viscera and a large exposed field, EVAPORATIVE loss can rival all other mechanisms combined — but the evaporation distractor is deliberately scoped to the chlorhexidine prep 'as it dries' (correctly transient), so the key remains unambiguously best for board purposes; this is minor stem-design tension, not a defeating ambiguity. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545239/ resolves (StatPearls 'Hypothermia') and does describe all four heat-loss mechanisms qualitatively, supporting the core concept. It is a generic, weak citation — no percentages, no perioperative/intraoperative or neonatal-specific discussion — but it is genuinely on-topic and not fabricated/404/mismatched, so it does not rise to a reference problem. Both checks pass; keep.

stem: A 71-year-old man is scheduled for an elective open infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. His history includes a drug-eluting coronary stent placed 4 years ago (currently asymptomatic on aspirin and a statin), insulin-dependent type 2 diabetes mellitus, and chronic kidney disease with a serum creatinine of 2.4 mg/dL. He has no history of stroke or heart failure and can climb two flights of stairs without chest pain or dyspnea. Using the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (Lee index), how many risk predictors does this patient have, and what is his approximate associated risk of a major adverse cardiac event?

choices.text: 2 predictors; approximately 10% risk
choices.rationale: This undercounts his predictors by omitting either the renal or surgical criterion, so both the count and the estimated risk are too low.

choices.text: 3 predictors; approximately 6% risk
choices.rationale: Although 3 predictors is closer, an RCRI of 3 or more carries a greater-than-15% MACE risk, not the 6% figure that corresponds to a score of 1.

choices.text: 4 predictors; greater than 15% risk
choices.rationale: He meets four criteria — ischemic heart disease (prior stent), insulin-dependent diabetes, serum creatinine over 2 mg/dL, and high-risk (suprainguinal/intraperitoneal) surgery — placing him in the highest RCRI category with greater than 15% MACE risk.

choices.text: 5 predictors; greater than 15% risk
choices.rationale: He does not have cerebrovascular disease or congestive heart failure, so only four of the six RCRI criteria are met, not five.

choices.text: 1 predictor; approximately 6% risk
choices.rationale: Counting only the coronary stent ignores his insulin-dependent diabetes, elevated creatinine, and high-risk surgery, all of which independently score in the RCRI.

explanation: The RCRI assigns one point each to ischemic heart disease, congestive heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, serum creatinine greater than 2 mg/dL, and high-risk surgery (suprainguinal vascular, intraperitoneal, or intrathoracic). This patient meets four of these. A remote, asymptomatic, optimized coronary stent still counts as ischemic heart disease, and only insulin-requiring diabetes — not diet- or oral-agent-controlled diabetes — qualifies. A score of 3 or more corresponds to a greater-than-15% rate of major adverse cardiac events.
subtopic: Preoperative cardiac risk stratification (Revised Cardiac Risk Index)

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Keyed answer (index 2: "4 predictors; >15% risk") is the single best answer. Patient meets exactly 4 of 6 RCRI criteria: (1) ischemic heart disease — prior drug-eluting coronary stent qualifies even though remote/asymptomatic/optimized; (2) insulin-dependent DM; (3) serum Cr 2.4 >2 mg/dL; (4) high-risk surgery — open infrarenal AAA repair is suprainguinal/intraperitoneal vascular surgery. He correctly does NOT score for CHF or cerebrovascular disease (none in history). RCRI score >=3 corresponds to >15% MACE. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: each carries a wrong predictor count, and the embedded risk figures correctly map to the wrong scores (6%=score 1, 10%=score 2), reinforcing the trap. The "two flights of stairs/>=4 METs" detail is a deliberate, valid distractor — functional capacity is not part of the RCRI, and the stem explicitly asks only about the RCRI. Explanation accurately states RCRI's six criteria and that only insulin-requiring (not oral/diet-controlled) diabetes counts.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537146/ resolves to "Cardiac Risk Assessment" (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf). It has a dedicated RCRI section listing all six predictors verbatim and states the exact risk bands used in the question: score 0=3.9%, 1=6.0%, 2=10.1%, >=3 = >15% MACE. Citation is real, on-topic, and directly supports both the criteria and the percentages. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 54-year-old woman presents for an urgent open reduction and internal fixation of a displaced ankle fracture sustained in a fall. She reports eating a full meal roughly 5 hours ago, but the timing is uncertain and she has type 2 diabetes with known gastroparesis. Surgery cannot be safely delayed much longer because of the fracture. To better characterize her aspiration risk before choosing an induction technique, the anesthetist performs a point-of-care gastric ultrasound with the patient in the right lateral decubitus position. The gastric antrum appears distended with a heterogeneous, 'frosted-glass' echogenic content. Based on this finding, what is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Proceed with a standard intravenous induction and bag-mask ventilation, since 5 hours have elapsed since the meal
choices.rationale: The sonographic finding of solid content overrides the elapsed-time estimate, and a routine induction without airway protection would expose her to a high aspiration risk.

choices.text: Treat the stomach as full and perform a rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure and a cuffed endotracheal tube
choices.rationale: A heterogeneous 'frosted-glass' antrum indicates solid gastric content, confirming a full stomach and high aspiration risk, which is the classic indication for rapid sequence induction with a protected airway.

choices.text: Place a laryngeal mask airway, as the gastroparesis makes endotracheal intubation unnecessary
choices.rationale: A supraglottic device does not protect against aspiration of a full stomach, so it is inappropriate when ultrasound demonstrates solid gastric content.

choices.text: Interpret the frosted-glass appearance as clear fluid and calculate gastric volume to decide whether to proceed
choices.rationale: Clear fluid appears hypoechoic (dark), whereas a heterogeneous frosted-glass echotexture signifies solids, so this misreads the image and the volume formula validated for clear fluid does not apply.

choices.text: Cancel the case outright and reschedule after an 8-hour fast
choices.rationale: Because the fracture repair is urgent and cannot be safely deferred, the correct response is to protect the airway rather than to cancel an indicated operation.

explanation: Point-of-care gastric ultrasound assesses the antrum, ideally in the right lateral decubitus position, to distinguish an empty antrum, clear fluid (hypoechoic), thick fluid, or solid content. A distended antrum with a heterogeneous 'frosted-glass' appearance indicates solid material and a full stomach regardless of reported fasting time, which is especially relevant in diabetic gastroparesis where gastric emptying is delayed. For an urgent case that cannot be deferred, the safest approach is a rapid sequence induction with a cuffed endotracheal tube. The validated clear-fluid volume formula applies only to anechoic fluid, not to solids.
subtopic: Point-of-care gastric ultrasound for aspiration risk assessment

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. A distended antrum with heterogeneous 'frosted-glass' echotexture on gastric POCUS indicates solid content (full stomach) regardless of the reported 5-hour interval, reinforced by diabetic gastroparesis; for an urgent, non-deferrable fracture repair the correct step is RSI with a cuffed ETT (keyed answer, index 1). All distractors are genuinely inferior: standard induction+BMV ignores a positive sonographic finding; an LMA does not protect a full stomach; reading frosted-glass as clear fluid is a misread (clear fluid is hypoechoic/anechoic and only that fluid is valid for the Perlas volume formula); cancellation is wrong for an urgent case. Per-choice rationales and the explanation are accurate and current per Perlas/Van de Putte gastric ultrasound literature. The only minor nuance (ongoing debate over cricoid pressure) does not affect correctness, as it remains the classic board-expected component of RSI. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK580524/ resolves to 'Gastric Ultrasound' (StatPearls) and is squarely on-topic, explicitly covering antrum assessment, right lateral decubitus positioning, the 'frosted-glass' appearance of solids, and the hypoechoic appearance of clear fluid. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 24-year-old man is brought to the OR for evacuation of an acute subdural hematoma after a motorcycle collision. He is intubated and sedated; an intraparenchymal monitor reads an intracranial pressure (ICP) of 24 mm Hg. Arterial blood gas shows PaCO2 28 mm Hg, and his mean arterial pressure (MAP) is 65 mm Hg with a cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) of 41 mm Hg. The neurosurgeon notes a tight, bulging brain on dural opening. Recognizing that pressure autoregulation is frequently impaired after severe traumatic brain injury, which intervention most appropriately addresses the inadequate cerebral perfusion in this patient?

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia and further lower PaCO2 to 22 mm Hg to maximize cerebral vasoconstriction
choices.rationale: Profound hypocapnia (PaCO2 < 25 mm Hg) causes excessive vasoconstriction that can produce cerebral ischemia and is not recommended; it would worsen, not improve, perfusion.

choices.text: Initiate a vasopressor (e.g., norepinephrine) to raise MAP toward a CPP of approximately 60 mm Hg
choices.rationale: With CPP of only 41 mm Hg and likely impaired autoregulation, augmenting MAP to restore CPP toward the recommended 60-70 mm Hg range is the most direct way to improve cerebral perfusion.

choices.text: Administer a 250 mL bolus of 0.45% saline to expand intravascular volume
choices.rationale: Hypotonic fluids worsen cerebral edema and ICP and provide little durable MAP support, making this harmful in a patient with intracranial hypertension.

choices.text: Allow PaCO2 to rise to 50 mm Hg to increase cerebral blood flow
choices.rationale: Permissive hypercapnia is a cerebral vasodilator that raises cerebral blood volume and ICP, which would worsen the already tight brain and lower CPP further.

choices.text: Begin a nitroprusside infusion to reduce afterload and improve cerebral blood flow
choices.rationale: Direct cerebral vasodilators like nitroprusside increase cerebral blood volume/ICP and lower MAP, reducing CPP—exactly the opposite of what is needed.

explanation: After severe TBI, pressure autoregulation is often impaired, so cerebral blood flow becomes pressure-passive and dependent on CPP (MAP minus ICP). Brain Trauma Foundation guidance targets a CPP of roughly 60-70 mm Hg; with a CPP of 41 mm Hg here, raising MAP with a vasopressor is the most appropriate maneuver. PaCO2 governs CO2 reactivity (roughly a 4% change in CBF per mm Hg) and is a separate axis—aggressive hyperventilation below 25 mm Hg risks ischemia, while hypercapnia raises ICP, so neither corrects the perfusion deficit.
subtopic: Cerebral autoregulation, CO2 reactivity, and CPP management in traumatic brain injury

verdict.reason: Clinical: Keyed answer (norepinephrine to raise MAP toward CPP ~60) is the single best option. CPP arithmetic is internally consistent (MAP 65 - ICP 24 = 41 mm Hg), well below the Brain Trauma Foundation target of 60-70, and with pressure-passive CBF after severe TBI, augmenting MAP is the most direct correction of the perfusion deficit. All four distractors are genuinely inferior, each wrong for a physiologically correct reason: profound hypocapnia <25 causes ischemia and does not fix perfusion; 0.45% saline is hypotonic and worsens edema/ICP; permissive hypercapnia to 50 raises CBV/ICP and worsens the tight brain; nitroprusside is a cerebral vasodilator that raises ICP and lowers MAP. The ~4%/mm Hg CO2 reactivity figure and the CPP=MAP-ICP relationship in the explanation are accurate and match the cited source. No ambiguity (ICP-directed therapies like osmotherapy are not among the offered choices, and the stem specifically asks about the perfusion deficit). Reference: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553183/ resolves to 'Physiology, Cerebral Autoregulation (StatPearls)', which covers cerebral autoregulation mechanisms, the CBF=(MAP-ICP)/CVR relationship, CO2 reactivity (~4%/mm Hg), and TBI CPP-target management. Title, URL, and topic all match the question.

stem: A 47-year-old woman with rapidly progressive Guillain-Barre syndrome (ascending weakness over 6 days, now requiring mechanical ventilation) is brought emergently to the OR for an open reduction of a femur fracture sustained in a fall. During induction her blood pressure swings from 180/100 to 80/45 over a few minutes and she has episodes of bradycardia. The anesthesiologist assistant is preparing the induction drugs. Which intraoperative consideration is most important in planning her anesthetic?

choices.text: Avoid succinylcholine because denervation-related upregulation of acetylcholine receptors predisposes to life-threatening hyperkalemia
choices.rationale: In GBS, functional denervation causes proliferation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine can trigger an exaggerated, potentially fatal potassium efflux and should be avoided.

choices.text: Use succinylcholine freely because the neuromuscular junction is already weak and additional blockade is harmless
choices.rationale: This is dangerous and incorrect; the upregulated receptors make succinylcholine-induced hyperkalemia more likely, not less, regardless of baseline weakness.

choices.text: Give a standard intubating dose of a nondepolarizing relaxant expecting a shortened, blunted duration of action
choices.rationale: GBS patients are typically more sensitive to nondepolarizing agents with prolonged blockade, so anticipating a shortened effect is wrong and risks residual paralysis.

choices.text: Plan for a stable, predictable hemodynamic course because autonomic involvement is rare in GBS
choices.rationale: Autonomic dysfunction occurs in roughly two-thirds of GBS patients and causes exactly the labile swings described, so assuming stability is incorrect and unsafe.

choices.text: Treat the labile hypertension aggressively with a long-acting antihypertensive infusion to keep pressures normalized
choices.rationale: Dysautonomic blood pressure swings are transient and exquisitely sensitive to drugs; long-acting agents can produce profound, sustained hypotension when the pressure spontaneously falls.

explanation: Guillain-Barre syndrome produces functional denervation with upregulation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors, so succinylcholine can cause exaggerated hyperkalemia and cardiac arrest and is contraindicated. These patients are also sensitive to nondepolarizing blockers with prolonged effect, and autonomic dysfunction (present in about two-thirds) causes the labile blood pressure and arrhythmias seen here—favoring short-acting, titratable agents over long-acting ones. Recognizing both the hyperkalemia risk and the dysautonomia is central to safe management.
subtopic: Guillain-Barre syndrome: dysautonomia and succinylcholine-induced hyperkalemia from denervation

verdict.reason: Clinical: Keyed answer A is correct and the single best answer. In GBS, functional denervation upregulates extrajunctional ACh receptors, making succinylcholine contraindicated due to risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia/cardiac arrest — a classic, current teaching. Distractors are all genuinely inferior: B (use sux freely) is dangerous and false; C (nondepolarizer shortened/blunted) is wrong since GBS patients are more sensitive with prolonged block; D (stable hemodynamics, autonomic involvement rare) is false — dysautonomia is common and explains the stem's labile BP/bradycardia; E (aggressive long-acting antihypertensive) is unsafe since dysautonomic swings are transient and risk profound hypotension. Only A is a true, safe statement, so no genuine second defensible answer exists; not ambiguous. The 'two-thirds' dysautonomia figure and nondepolarizer-sensitivity claims are accurate. Reference: WebFetch of the StatPearls 'Succinylcholine Chloride' page (NBK499984) resolves and is directly on-topic — it covers hyperkalemia as the most common adverse effect and explicitly attributes the danger to disease pathophysiology inducing upregulation of postjunctional ACh receptors (the exact keyed mechanism). GBS is not named by name, but the cited mechanism fully supports the question, so the citation is appropriate.

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 woman presents to the emergency department 3 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and bilateral leg edema. She has no prior cardiac history. Vital signs: HR 118, BP 102/68, RR 26, SpO2 91% on room air. Examination reveals jugular venous distension, bibasilar crackles, and an S3 gallop. A bedside echocardiogram shows a dilated left ventricle with an estimated ejection fraction of 28% and no valvular stenosis. She now requires urgent dilation and curettage for retained products of conception causing hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most appropriate consideration in planning her anesthetic?

choices.text: Spinal anesthesia with a standard hyperbaric bupivacaine dose is preferred because it provides the fastest reliable surgical block
choices.rationale: A single-shot spinal produces an abrupt sympathectomy and sudden drop in afterload and preload that is poorly tolerated by a failing, dilated ventricle dependent on stable loading conditions.

choices.text: Ephedrine is the vasopressor of choice if hypotension occurs because it preserves uteroplacental perfusion
choices.rationale: Uteroplacental perfusion is irrelevant postpartum, and ephedrine's beta-agonist chronotropy and inotropy are undesirable in a patient with tachycardia and a failing ventricle.

choices.text: The clinical picture is most consistent with an exacerbation of chronic pulmonary hypertension rather than a new cardiomyopathy
choices.rationale: A newly dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 28% arising within months of delivery in a previously healthy woman defines peripartum cardiomyopathy, not pulmonary hypertension.

choices.text: A carefully titrated technique that avoids tachycardia and abrupt changes in preload and afterload, with invasive monitoring and a vasopressor such as phenylephrine available, is most appropriate
choices.rationale: Peripartum cardiomyopathy with severe systolic dysfunction demands hemodynamic stability, avoidance of myocardial depressants and tachycardia, and an afterload-sparing pressor strategy with arterial monitoring.

choices.text: Bromocriptine should be administered intraoperatively to acutely improve her ejection fraction before surgery
choices.rationale: Bromocriptine is a disease-modifying adjunct to guideline-directed heart-failure therapy that works over days to weeks, not an acute intraoperative inotropic intervention.

explanation: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is idiopathic systolic heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction below 45% presenting in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, with most cases detected in the first month after delivery. The anesthetic priority is maintaining hemodynamic stability: avoiding tachycardia, preventing abrupt sympathectomy-induced falls in preload and afterload, and choosing a pressor like phenylephrine that supports systemic vascular resistance without excessive chronotropy. Bromocriptine (a prolactin-blocking dopamine agonist) is a disease-modifying adjunct, not an acute rescue agent.
subtopic: Maternal cardiac disease — peripartum cardiomyopathy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Stem is a textbook presentation of peripartum cardiomyopathy (previously healthy, 3 weeks postpartum, new dilated LV with EF 28%, decompensated HF) needing urgent D&C. Keyed answer D (titrated technique avoiding tachycardia and abrupt preload/afterload swings, invasive monitoring, afterload-sparing pressor available) is the single best anesthetic principle for severe systolic dysfunction. All four distractors carry a clear, falsifiable error: A — single-shot spinal causes abrupt sympathectomy poorly tolerated by a failing dilated LV; B — uteroplacental perfusion is irrelevant postpartum and ephedrine's chronotropy/inotropy is undesirable with HR 118 and a failing ventricle; C — a new dilated LV with EF 28% defines PPCM, not chronic pulmonary HTN (misdiagnosis); E — bromocriptine is a disease-modifying adjunct acting over days–weeks, not an acute intraoperative inotrope. One adversarial nuance: pure alpha-agonism with phenylephrine raises afterload, which some would replace with an inopressor in severe systolic failure; however D only states phenylephrine should be 'available' within a broader titrated/afterload-sparing strategy and phenylephrine's reflex bradycardia is actually helpful in this tachycardic patient, so D remains unambiguously best with no competing defensible option. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482185/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Peripartum Cardiomyopathy' article, which confirms the EF <45% definition, late-pregnancy/early-postpartum timing, and bromocriptine's prolactin/16-kDa mechanism as an adjunct. Citation is accurate and directly on-topic.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 39 weeks gestation in active labor requests epidural analgesia. She has gestational thrombocytopenia noted in the third trimester with no bleeding history, no hypertension, no antiplatelet or anticoagulant use, and a normal prothrombin time and activated partial thromboplastin time. Her most recent complete blood count, drawn 1 hour ago, shows a platelet count of 88,000/microL. She is contracting every 3 minutes and is in significant pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate course of action?

choices.text: Withhold all neuraxial techniques and offer only intravenous opioid analgesia because the platelet count is below 100,000/microL
choices.rationale: A threshold of 100,000/microL is outdated and overly conservative for obstetric patients and would unnecessarily deny effective analgesia to a stable parturient.

choices.text: Proceed with placement of the labor epidural, as her platelet count exceeds the threshold below which spinal-epidural hematoma risk becomes a significant concern in this setting
choices.rationale: In a parturient with gestational thrombocytopenia and no additional risk factors, a platelet count of at least 70,000/microL is associated with a very low risk of spinal-epidural hematoma, so neuraxial analgesia is reasonable.

choices.text: Transfuse one unit of apheresis platelets to raise the count above 100,000/microL before placing the epidural
choices.rationale: There is no evidence-based indication to transfuse platelets prophylactically at this count, and transfusion exposes the patient to unnecessary risk without proven benefit.

choices.text: Perform a single-shot spinal rather than an epidural because the smaller needle eliminates the hematoma risk
choices.rationale: Although a smaller needle is used, single-shot spinal does not eliminate hematoma risk and offers no decision advantage here; the platelet count itself already supports a neuraxial technique.

choices.text: Defer the decision until a thromboelastography study is performed to quantify clot strength
choices.rationale: Routine viscoelastic testing is not required to proceed in an otherwise stable parturient whose platelet count already falls in a reassuring range, and waiting needlessly delays analgesia.

explanation: The SOAP interdisciplinary consensus and OpenAnesthesia summarize that in obstetric patients with thrombocytopenia from gestational thrombocytopenia, immune thrombocytopenia, or hypertensive disorders, and no other risk factors, a platelet count of at least 70,000/microL carries a very low risk of spinal-epidural hematoma, making neuraxial procedures reasonable. The historically cited 100,000/microL cutoff is no longer supported as a hard limit. Normal coagulation studies and the absence of antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy further support proceeding.
subtopic: Neuraxial anesthesia in the thrombocytopenic parturient

verdict.reason:
CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Keyed answer (index 1, proceed with epidural) is correct. Per the 2021 SOAP interdisciplinary consensus statement, a platelet count >=70,000/microL in a parturient with gestational thrombocytopenia and no additional risk factors carries a very low risk of spinal-epidural hematoma (95% CI upper bound 0.19% for the 70,000-100,000 band), so neuraxial analgesia is reasonable. This patient is at 88,000/microL with a normal PT/aPTT, no bleeding history, no antiplatelet/anticoagulant use, and the count was drawn 1 hour ago (recent) - unambiguously in the reassuring range. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (0) the 100,000/microL hard cutoff is outdated; (2) prophylactic platelet transfusion is not indicated and OpenAnesthesia itself notes no transfusion recommendation below thresholds; (3) single-shot spinal does NOT eliminate hematoma risk - factually false; (4) routine TEG/viscoelastic testing is not required to proceed. Choice-1 rationale ("at least 70,000/microL... very low risk") matches SOAP wording exactly, and the explanation correctly attributes the threshold and notes the 100,000 cutoff is no longer a hard limit. No second defensible answer.

REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/platelets/ resolved (HTTP 200) and is directly on-topic. The page explicitly states "Platelets should be at least 70 x 10^9/L prior to obstetric neuraxial anesthesia or 80 x 10^9/L prior to nonobstetric neuraxial anesthesia" and cites the SOAP interdisciplinary consensus statement - precisely the claim the question hinges on. Citation supports the keyed answer.

Both clinical and reference checks pass; keep.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 woman at 31 weeks gestation is admitted in preterm labor and started on a continuous intravenous terbutaline infusion for tocolysis. Six hours later she develops dyspnea, tachycardia to 138 beats/min, and an SpO2 of 89% on room air; auscultation reveals new bilateral crackles and a chest radiograph shows diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates with a normal cardiac silhouette. Her serum potassium is 2.9 mEq/L and her glucose is 188 mg/dL. The obstetric team now plans an urgent cesarean delivery for non-reassuring fetal status. Which of the following statements best guides her anesthetic management?

choices.text: Terbutaline-induced bronchospasm is the cause of her hypoxemia, so escalating the terbutaline dose will improve oxygenation
choices.rationale: Her hypoxemia is from beta-agonist-associated pulmonary edema, not bronchospasm, and increasing terbutaline would worsen the maternal cardiopulmonary and metabolic derangements.

choices.text: The hypokalemia reflects a true total-body potassium deficit that must be fully corrected with aggressive potassium replacement before induction
choices.rationale: Beta-agonist hypokalemia is largely from intracellular potassium shift rather than true depletion, so aggressive replacement risks rebound hyperkalemia as the drug effect resolves.

choices.text: Beta-agonist tocolytics reliably reduce maternal heart rate, so her tachycardia indicates an unrelated cause such as sepsis
choices.rationale: Beta-agonists characteristically cause maternal tachycardia, so her elevated heart rate is an expected and direct drug effect, not evidence of an unrelated process.

choices.text: Neuraxial anesthesia is absolutely contraindicated because the sympathectomy will precipitate cardiovascular collapse in any patient on a tocolytic
choices.rationale: Neuraxial anesthesia is not absolutely contraindicated; with appropriate fluid and hemodynamic management it is often preferable, and a blanket contraindication is incorrect.

choices.text: Discontinuing the terbutaline, supporting oxygenation, and anticipating residual maternal tachycardia and hyperglycemia are key priorities as delivery proceeds
choices.rationale: Stopping the offending beta-agonist while supporting oxygenation addresses the pulmonary edema, and recognizing the persistent tachycardia and hyperglycemia from beta-receptor stimulation guides safe anesthetic conduct.

explanation: Beta-sympathomimetic tocolytics such as terbutaline cause maternal tachycardia, hypokalemia from intracellular potassium shift, hyperglycemia, and a recognized risk of pulmonary edema, which contributed to FDA warnings and reclassification of the drug. The first management step is to discontinue the offending agent and support oxygenation; the hypokalemia is a transient shift that does not require aggressive repletion and may rebound once the drug is stopped. Anesthetic planning should anticipate the residual beta-agonist effects rather than attributing the tachycardia to an unrelated cause.
subtopic: Tocolytic pharmacology — beta-agonist (terbutaline) maternal effects

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 4 — discontinue terbutaline, support oxygenation, anticipate residual beta-agonist tachycardia and hyperglycemia) is the single best, unambiguous answer. The clinical picture (dyspnea, hypoxemia, bilateral crackles, diffuse interstitial infiltrates with a normal cardiac silhouette after a continuous IV beta-agonist) is classic non-cardiogenic/permeability pulmonary edema from beta-sympathomimetic tocolysis, with the expected metabolic triad of hypokalemia (intracellular shift), hyperglycemia, and tachycardia. All four distractors are genuinely and clearly inferior: A is dangerously wrong (it is edema, not bronchospasm; escalating terbutaline worsens everything); B is wrong because beta-agonist hypokalemia is a transient transcellular shift that rebounds on drug cessation, so aggressive repletion risks hyperkalemia; C is wrong because beta-agonists characteristically cause tachycardia (not bradycardia), so the tachycardia is an expected drug effect; D is wrong because neuraxial anesthesia is not absolutely contraindicated. Every per-choice rationale and the explanation are accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK562212/ resolves to StatPearls 'Tocolysis,' which is squarely on-topic — it covers beta-agonist tocolytics including terbutaline, the FDA black box warning on injectable terbutaline, and links beta-agonist/ritodrine tocolytics to pulmonary edema. The page does not explicitly enumerate hypokalemia/hyperglycemia or name terbutaline (vs ritodrine) for pulmonary edema, but these are well-established textbook effects of beta-sympathomimetics and the citation is a valid, on-topic supporting reference (not fabricated, 404, or mismatched). Both dimensions sound.

stem: A 34-year-old woman (68 kg, nonsmoker) presents for outpatient laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anesthesia. She reports severe seasickness on cruises and vomited after a prior wisdom-tooth extraction under sedation. The surgeon plans to use intraoperative and postoperative opioids for pain. The anesthesia care team wants to minimize her risk of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) and unplanned admission. Based on her Apfel simplified risk score, which strategy is most appropriate?

choices.text: She has 1 risk factor (low risk); a single agent such as dexamethasone is sufficient
choices.rationale: Incorrect because she actually has four Apfel risk factors, placing her in the high-risk category that warrants combination prophylaxis, not a single agent.

choices.text: She has 4 risk factors (high risk, ~80%); give 2-3 prophylactic agents from different classes plus a propofol-based, opioid-sparing technique
choices.rationale: Correct because female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV/motion sickness, and planned postoperative opioids are all four Apfel factors, predicting roughly 80% risk and justifying multimodal combination prophylaxis with reduced baseline-risk strategies.

choices.text: She has 2 risk factors (moderate risk); withhold all prophylaxis and treat with ondansetron only if she vomits in the PACU
choices.rationale: Incorrect because she has four (not two) risk factors, and rescue-only treatment is inferior to prophylaxis in high-risk patients where prevention is more effective than waiting for symptoms.

choices.text: Her smoking history is unknown so the score cannot be calculated; proceed without PONV planning
choices.rationale: Incorrect because the stem explicitly identifies her as a nonsmoker, and an incomplete history would never justify omitting PONV risk reduction.

choices.text: She has 3 risk factors; a single dose of ondansetron at the end of surgery is adequate monotherapy
choices.rationale: Incorrect on both the count (four factors, not three) and the plan, since high-risk patients require combination prophylaxis rather than a single 5-HT3 antagonist.

explanation: The Apfel simplified score awards one point each for female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV or motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioid use; this patient meets all four, predicting approximately an 80% incidence of PONV. Consensus guidelines recommend matching the number of interventions to the risk: high-risk patients should receive two or more antiemetics from different pharmacologic classes combined with baseline-risk-reduction measures such as a propofol-based total intravenous technique and opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia. In the ambulatory setting, effective PONV prophylaxis directly reduces delayed discharge and unplanned admission.
subtopic: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) risk stratification and prophylaxis

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (index 1) is the single best answer. Apfel simplified score awards one point each for female sex, nonsmoking status, history of PONV OR motion sickness, and anticipated postoperative opioid use. This patient has all four (female, nonsmoker, motion sickness/prior PONV grouped as one factor, planned opioids) = 4 points, predicting ~80% PONV risk. The question correctly counts these as 4 (does not erroneously double-count motion sickness and prior PONV as separate points). Management is consistent with the 4th Consensus Guidelines (Gan et al., 2020): high-risk patients should receive >=2 antiemetics from different classes plus baseline risk reduction (propofol-based TIVA, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia). All distractors are genuinely inferior: indices 0, 2, and 4 use wrong factor counts and undertreat; index 3 is directly contradicted by the stem (explicitly nonsmoker) and proposes abandoning planning. Rationales and explanation are accurate and current. The ambulatory framing (PONV prophylaxis reducing delayed discharge/unplanned admission) is correct. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK500029/ resolves to a real StatPearls chapter titled 'Postoperative Nausea' (updated Nov 2022). It discusses PONV risk factors (nonsmoking females with history of PONV/motion sickness, postoperative opioids), the ~80% incidence with 4 risk factors, combination/multimodal prophylaxis, and prevention being superior to rescue treatment. The citation is on-topic and supports the question.

stem: A 5-year-old boy (20 kg) who underwent an adenotonsillectomy 6 hours earlier at an ambulatory surgery center returns with active bright-red oral bleeding and several episodes of vomiting dark blood. He is pale and anxious, with heart rate 148/min, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, and capillary refill of 3 seconds; the surgeon plans an emergent return to the operating room for control of post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage. Which induction approach is most appropriate?

choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane in a spontaneously breathing patient, keeping him deep before laryngoscopy to avoid an intravenous line
choices.rationale: Incorrect because a slow inhalational induction in a hypovolemic patient with a stomach full of blood markedly increases aspiration and hemodynamic-collapse risk; intravenous access and rapid airway protection are required.

choices.text: Establish or confirm IV access, begin volume resuscitation, then perform a rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure using a reduced dose of induction agent and a second functioning suction ready
choices.rationale: Correct because the child should be treated as a hypovolemic full stomach; volume resuscitation before induction, a rapid sequence technique with cricoid pressure, dose-reduced induction agents to limit hemodynamic collapse, and dual suction address the principal risks of aspiration and cardiovascular instability.

choices.text: Proceed immediately with a full-dose propofol rapid sequence induction without fluid resuscitation, since any delay risks further blood loss
choices.rationale: Incorrect because a full induction dose in an unresuscitated, hypovolemic child can precipitate severe hypotension or arrest; induction agents should be dose-reduced and resuscitation begun first.

choices.text: Awake fiberoptic intubation without sedation to preserve airway reflexes throughout
choices.rationale: Incorrect because awake fiberoptic intubation is impractical and unsafe in a frightened, actively bleeding child whose airway view would be obscured by blood, delaying definitive control.

choices.text: Defer surgery for at least 6 hours of strict NPO to ensure gastric emptying before any anesthetic
choices.rationale: Incorrect because active hemorrhage is a surgical emergency, the stomach can never be assumed empty regardless of NPO time, and delay risks ongoing blood loss and decompensation.

explanation: A child with post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage must be managed as both hypovolemic and full of swallowed blood, since significant blood loss can be masked until roughly 40% of blood volume is lost. The correct sequence is to obtain reliable IV access and begin volume resuscitation, then perform a rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure using judiciously reduced induction-agent doses to avoid hemodynamic collapse, with a second suction immediately available because blood often obscures the laryngeal view. Gastric emptying with an orogastric tube and careful suctioning before emergence further reduce aspiration risk.
subtopic: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage in the ambulatory pediatric patient

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The keyed answer (B) — confirm IV access, begin volume resuscitation, then RSI with cricoid pressure using a reduced induction-agent dose and a second suction ready — is the single best, textbook-correct management of pediatric post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage, treating the child as hypovolemic + full stomach. All four distractors are genuinely inferior: (A) slow spontaneous inhalational induction increases aspiration/collapse risk and forgoes needed IV access; (C) full-dose propofol without resuscitation risks hemodynamic collapse/arrest; (D) awake fiberoptic without sedation is impractical/unsafe in a frightened, actively bleeding child with a blood-obscured view; (E) deferring for NPO is wrong because active hemorrhage is an emergency and the stomach is never assumed empty. Rationales and the explanation (including the ~40% blood-volume-loss masking point and OG decompression/suction before emergence) are accurate and current. The only minor nuance — ongoing debate about cricoid pressure efficacy — does not make the key wrong or elevate any distractor. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/posttonsillectomy-hemorrhage/ resolved successfully (active OpenAnesthesia page, updated Feb 2023) and is precisely on-topic, explicitly supporting resuscitation-first, RSI with reduced dosing, two working suctions, the 40% blood-volume figure, and OG decompression. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 71-year-old man (98 kg) undergoes a total knee arthroplasty under general anesthesia with a pneumatic thigh tourniquet inflated to 300 mmHg. The tourniquet has been up for 105 minutes. Ventilation has been stable at a tidal volume of 600 mL, rate 10/min, with end-tidal CO2 (EtCO2) running 36-38 mmHg. Immediately after the surgeon deflates the tourniquet to begin closure, the anesthetist observes the EtCO2 climb to 49 mmHg over the next 90 seconds, heart rate rise from 72 to 96/min, and a transient dip in systolic blood pressure from 128 to 108 mmHg. SpO2 remains 99%. Which mechanism best explains the rise in EtCO2 immediately following tourniquet release?

choices.text: Washout of CO2 and acid metabolites that accumulated in the ischemic limb back into the central circulation
choices.rationale: Correct: during ischemia the isolated limb accumulates CO2, lactate, and other acid metabolites, and on deflation these are flushed into the systemic circulation, transiently raising mixed venous and arterial CO2 delivered to the lungs and increasing EtCO2.

choices.text: A subclinical venous air embolism entrained through the open femoral marrow during cement pressurization
choices.rationale: Incorrect: venous air or fat embolism characteristically causes a sudden DROP in EtCO2 from increased alveolar dead space, not the rise seen here.

choices.text: Acute malignant hyperthermia triggered by the volatile anesthetic, with hypermetabolic CO2 production
choices.rationale: Incorrect: MH produces a sustained, progressive EtCO2 rise with tachycardia, rigidity, and hyperthermia rather than a transient bump timed precisely to tourniquet deflation, and it is not provoked by tourniquet release.

choices.text: Abrupt hypoventilation from a decrease in minute ventilation as the surgical stimulus diminished
choices.rationale: Incorrect: minute ventilation was unchanged (fixed tidal volume and rate on the ventilator), so hypoventilation cannot account for the rise.

choices.text: Increased dead-space ventilation from pulmonary vasoconstriction reducing CO2 elimination
choices.rationale: Incorrect: increased dead space reduces the EtCO2 relative to arterial CO2 (a falling EtCO2 pattern), which is the opposite of the observed climb.

explanation: Prolonged limb ischemia under a tourniquet causes accumulation of CO2, lactate, and potassium in the isolated extremity. On deflation, this acidotic, CO2-rich blood is washed into the central circulation, transiently increasing the CO2 load delivered to the lungs and raising EtCO2 (often with a CO2 production spike of up to ~80% within two minutes), along with a brief tachycardia and mild hypotension from reactive hyperemia and the metabolite bolus. The change is self-limited over roughly 10-30 minutes; increasing minute ventilation briefly helps clear the load. A falling EtCO2 should instead prompt evaluation for embolism.
subtopic: Orthopedic anesthesia: pneumatic tourniquet physiology and deflation effects

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (washout of CO2/lactate/acid metabolites from the ischemic limb into central circulation on deflation) is the single best, textbook-correct mechanism for the post-tourniquet EtCO2 rise, and the accompanying transient tachycardia and mild hypotension fit reactive hyperemia/metabolite bolus. All four distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: VAE/fat embolism causes an EtCO2 DROP (dead space), not a rise; MH gives a sustained progressive rise not timed to deflation and is not provoked by tourniquet release; minute ventilation is fixed on the ventilator so hypoventilation is excluded; increased dead space lowers EtCO2 relative to PaCO2 (opposite of observed). The stem numbers are internally consistent (EtCO2 peak over ~90 sec matches the literature's ~1-minute peak). Only trivial, non-load-bearing imprecisions: the explanation's '~80% VCO2 spike' figure and the '10-30 minutes' self-limited timeframe blend EtCO2 peak/return (~10 min) with the duration of metabolic acidosis (10-30 min) — defensible, not erroneous. Answer is unambiguous. REFERENCE: WebFetch of the OpenAnesthesia 'Perioperative Tourniquet Use' page resolves and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating 'Tourniquet deflation allows for CO2-rich venous blood from the ischemic limb to enter systemic circulation. EtCO2 levels peak at 1 minute and return to baseline levels in 10 minutes,' plus potassium/lactate washout, transient BP reduction, and metabolic acidosis — fully supporting the keyed mechanism and distractor logic. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes monopolar transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) under spinal anesthesia for refractory benign prostatic hyperplasia. The irrigant is 1.5% glycine. Approximately 50 minutes into a difficult resection with several opened venous sinuses, he becomes restless and complains that the room looks dim and blurry, then reports nausea. His blood pressure is 158/92 mmHg, heart rate 54/min, and SpO2 96%. A stat sodium returns at 118 mmol/L. Which feature of this presentation is most specifically attributable to the irrigating solution used rather than to the hyponatremia alone?

choices.text: The bradycardia and systolic hypertension
choices.rationale: Incorrect: hypertension with reflex bradycardia reflects fluid overload and rising intracranial pressure from the absorbed volume and hyponatremia, not a glycine-specific effect.

choices.text: The transient visual disturbance / blurred, dim vision
choices.rationale: Correct: glycine is an inhibitory neurotransmitter in the retina, and its absorption (along with its metabolite ammonia) produces transient visual disturbances and even transient blindness that are characteristic of glycine irrigant and distinct from the hyponatremic encephalopathy itself.

choices.text: The serum sodium of 118 mmol/L
choices.rationale: Incorrect: dilutional hyponatremia results from absorption of any hypotonic irrigant and is not specific to glycine.

choices.text: The nausea and restlessness
choices.rationale: Incorrect: nausea and altered mentation are nonspecific manifestations of acute hyponatremia and volume overload, seen regardless of which hypotonic irrigant is used.

choices.text: The fall in SpO2 toward 96%
choices.rationale: Incorrect: mild desaturation reflects pulmonary congestion from intravascular volume overload, a generic consequence of irrigant absorption rather than a glycine-specific phenomenon.

explanation: TURP syndrome is the constellation of dilutional hyponatremia, hypervolemia, and tissue edema from systemic absorption of hypotonic irrigation through open prostatic venous sinuses; it complicates roughly 10-15% of monopolar TURPs. Most features (hyponatremia, hypertension with bradycardia, pulmonary congestion, encephalopathy) stem from the absorbed water and falling sodium and would occur with any hypotonic irrigant. The transient visual disturbance is the glycine-specific clue: glycine is an inhibitory retinal neurotransmitter and is metabolized to ammonia, producing blurring, dimming, or transient blindness. Management centers on stopping resection, supporting ventilation, and correcting symptomatic hyponatremia (hypertonic saline for severe neurologic signs).
subtopic: Urologic anesthesia: TURP syndrome and glycine irrigant absorption
reference_title: Hyponatremia and Transurethral Resection of Prostate Syndrome - OpenAnesthesia

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (transient visual disturbance/dim-blurred vision) is unambiguously the single best answer. Glycine is the principal inhibitory neurotransmitter of the retina, and transient visual disturbance/transient blindness is a classic glycine-specific manifestation of TURP syndrome that is distinct from hyponatremic encephalopathy; the rationale's mention of glycine-to-ammonia metabolism as a contributor is also accurate. The four distractors are all genuinely inferior because each (hyponatremia, nausea/restlessness, hypertension with bradycardia, mild desaturation) occurs with absorption of ANY hypotonic irrigant and is not glycine-specific. Only the visual disturbance and hyperammonemic encephalopathy are glycine-specific, and only the former is offered, so there is no competing correct answer. The explanation is accurate and current: TURP syndrome incidence of 10-15% matches the reference, and the pathophysiology and management (stop resection, support ventilation, hypertonic saline for severe neurologic signs) are correct. Minor non-issue: the stem uses bradycardia (HR 54) while the cited page lists tachycardia as a typical early sign, but bradycardia is physiologically defensible as a Cushing-type reflex from acute cerebral edema/rising ICP, it is correctly keyed as a distractor, and it does not compete with the answer. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/keywords/hyponatermia-and-transurethral-resection-of-prostate-syndrome/ resolves successfully and is directly on-topic — it covers TURP syndrome, dilutional hyponatremia, 1.5% glycine irrigant, hyperammonemia from glycine degradation, and explicitly attributes transient visual disturbances to glycine absorption rather than hyponatremia alone, fully supporting the keyed answer. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 44-year-old woman undergoes operative hysteroscopy with a resectoscope for a submucosal fibroid under general anesthesia with a laryngeal mask airway. CO2 is being used for uterine distention. About 20 minutes into the case, while the surgeon is actively resecting, the EtCO2 abruptly falls from 35 mmHg to 19 mmHg, SpO2 drops to 88%, and the blood pressure declines from 118/74 to 78/45 mmHg with a heart rate of 124/min. A harsh churning sound is heard on auscultation over the precordium. Ventilation through the LMA meets no increased resistance and the airway pressures are unchanged. Which is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Tell the surgeon to stop gas insufflation and flood the surgical field with saline, and place the patient in left lateral decubitus, head-down position
choices.rationale: Correct: the picture is venous gas embolism from CO2 entrainment through open uterine venous channels, and the priority is to halt further gas entry (stop insufflation, flood the field) and reposition into left lateral head-down (Durant) position to trap gas in the right ventricular apex away from the outflow tract.

choices.text: Administer a 1.5 mg/kg bolus of intravenous lidocaine to treat presumed local-anesthetic systemic toxicity
choices.rationale: Incorrect: no local anesthetic was administered, this is not LAST, and lidocaine would not address gas embolism.

choices.text: Deepen anesthesia and increase the uterine distention pressure to improve surgical visualization
choices.rationale: Incorrect: raising intrauterine pressure increases the gradient driving gas into open venous sinuses and would worsen the embolism.

choices.text: Treat for anaphylaxis with intramuscular epinephrine and stop all colloids
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the abrupt EtCO2 fall with a mill-wheel murmur points to gas embolism, not anaphylaxis, and there is no urticaria, bronchospasm, or new drug exposure.

choices.text: Switch from CO2 to room-air insufflation to reduce absorption-related acidosis
choices.rationale: Incorrect: insufflating air would be far more dangerous because nitrogen is poorly soluble and far less rapidly absorbed than CO2; the answer is to stop insufflation, not change the gas.

explanation: Operative hysteroscopy opens large venous sinuses in the myometrium, and gas (CO2 from insufflation or from electrosurgical vaporization) can be entrained into the venous circulation. Venous gas embolism classically presents with a sudden fall in EtCO2 (increased alveolar dead space), hypoxemia, hypotension, tachycardia, and a mill-wheel (churning) murmur. Immediate management is to stop the gas source and flood the field, give 100% oxygen, support the circulation, and position the patient left lateral head-down (Durant maneuver) to keep gas in the right ventricular apex; aspiration through a central catheter may be attempted in severe cases. CO2 is preferred over air precisely because of its high blood solubility and rapid resorption.
subtopic: Gynecologic anesthesia: venous gas (CO2) embolism during operative hysteroscopy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem is a textbook presentation of venous CO2 gas embolism during operative hysteroscopy: sudden EtCO2 fall (35→19, dead-space effect), hypoxemia, hypotension, tachycardia, and a mill-wheel/churning precordial murmur, with unchanged airway pressures (excluding airway obstruction/bronchospasm). The keyed answer (index 0) correctly prioritizes halting gas entry (stop insufflation, flood the field) and adds the classically taught Durant maneuver (left lateral decubitus, head-down). All distractors are genuinely inferior: no local anesthetic was given (lidocaine/LAST is irrelevant), raising distention pressure worsens the driving gradient and is dangerous, anaphylaxis would not produce an abrupt EtCO2 fall with a mill-wheel murmur and there is no trigger/urticaria/bronchospasm, and switching CO2 to room air is more dangerous because poorly soluble nitrogen resorbs slowly. The explanation about CO2 being preferred over air for its high solubility is accurate and current. No second defensible answer.\n\nREFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.openanesthesia.org/venous_air_embolism/ resolves successfully and is genuinely on-topic — it covers VAE pathophysiology, presentation (rapid EtCO2 fall, mill-wheel murmur, tachycardia), and management (prevent further air entry, surgeon notification, flood field, 100% O2, vasopressors/inotropes). It does not explicitly name the Durant/left-lateral-head-down position, but it supports the core management taught in the answer; this is an acceptable supporting citation, not a mismatch. Both clinical and reference are sound.

stem: A 58-year-old woman with COPD (baseline FEV1 55% predicted, on home oxygen at 2 L/min) presents for arthroscopic rotator cuff repair of the right shoulder. The anesthesiologist plans an ultrasound-guided single-injection interscalene brachial plexus block with 20 mL of 0.5% ropivacaine for postoperative analgesia. Twenty minutes after the block, in the preoperative holding area, the patient reports new shortness of breath and a sensation that she cannot take a full breath. SpO2 has fallen from 94% to 87% on her home oxygen, she is tachypneic, but she is speaking in full sentences and the surgical site is well anesthetized. Lung auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds at the right base without wheezing. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her respiratory deterioration?

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity from intravascular ropivacaine
choices.rationale: LAST presents with CNS prodrome (perioral numbness, tinnitus, agitation, seizures) and cardiovascular instability rather than isolated dyspnea with a unilaterally diminished hemithorax, and 20 mL of ropivacaine deposited correctly is not a toxic systemic load.

choices.text: Ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis
choices.rationale: Correct: the phrenic nerve lies on the anterior scalene immediately deep to the interscalene injection site, so anterior local anesthetic spread produces ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis in essentially 100% of conventional interscalene blocks, which is clinically significant in a patient with limited pulmonary reserve.

choices.text: Pneumothorax from pleural puncture during needle advancement
choices.rationale: Pneumothorax is far less common with the interscalene approach than with supraclavicular blocks because the needle stays cephalad to the pleural dome, and it would typically produce absent (not merely diminished basal) breath sounds with hyperresonance.

choices.text: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block producing vocal cord dysfunction
choices.rationale: Recurrent laryngeal nerve block causes hoarseness and, in patients with contralateral cord palsy, airway obstruction with stridor, not basal hypoventilation with a fall in SpO2 and preserved phonation in full sentences.

choices.text: Acute bronchospasm triggered by a COPD exacerbation
choices.rationale: Bronchospasm would produce diffuse expiratory wheezing and prolonged expiration rather than unilaterally diminished breath sounds, and the tight temporal link to a recently performed interscalene block points instead to phrenic involvement.

explanation: The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) runs along the anterior scalene muscle just anterior to the interscalene groove, so local anesthetic deposited for an interscalene block almost universally spreads to it, producing ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis and roughly a 25-30% reduction in pulmonary function. Healthy patients tolerate this, but those with significant pulmonary disease (severe COPD, contralateral diaphragm dysfunction) can develop clinically important hypoventilation, making the interscalene block a relative contraindication in this population. Lower local anesthetic volumes, more distal approaches (e.g., superior trunk block), and ultrasound guidance reduce but do not eliminate the risk.
subtopic: Interscalene block complications - phrenic nerve / hemidiaphragmatic paresis

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: The keyed answer (B, ipsilateral phrenic nerve block causing hemidiaphragmatic paresis) is unambiguously the single best answer. The vignette is textbook: dyspnea/hypoxia ~20 min after a conventional-volume (20 mL 0.5% ropivacaine) interscalene block, ipsilateral (right) basal diminished breath sounds from an elevated paralyzed hemidiaphragm, well-anesthetized surgical site confirming successful local-anesthetic spread, no wheezing, no CNS/CV prodrome, preserved phonation. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and their rationales are accurate: A (LAST) lacks CNS prodrome/CV instability and a toxic load; C (pneumothorax) is rare with the cephalad interscalene approach and would give absent breath sounds with hyperresonance; D (RLN block) causes hoarseness/stridor not basal hypoventilation, and the patient is phonating fully; E (bronchospasm) would give diffuse wheezing, not unilateral diminished sounds. The explanation's figures (near-universal diaphragmatic paresis with conventional volumes; ~25-30% reduction in pulmonary function; relative contraindication in significant pulmonary disease; risk reduced but not eliminated by low volume, more distal/superior-trunk approaches, and ultrasound) are correct and current. The "essentially 100%" claim is appropriate for the 20 mL conventional volume used, and the explanation appropriately notes mitigation. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519491/ resolves to StatPearls 'Interscalene Block,' directly on-topic, explicitly stating it is 'contraindicated in patients with respiratory insufficiency due to the high likelihood of ipsilateral phrenic nerve block and diaphragmatic hemiparesis, which can lead to a 25% reduction in pulmonary function.' Citation is valid and corroborates the key. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 64-year-old man with hypertension undergoes an open sigmoid colectomy for diverticular disease under general anesthesia. The surgeon requests a bilateral ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane (TAP) block placed at the end of the case as part of a multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesic plan. The blocks are performed correctly with appropriate local anesthetic spread between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles. In the post-anesthesia care unit, the patient reports that his incisional (skin and abdominal wall) pain is well controlled, but he is experiencing significant deep, cramping, diffuse abdominal pain that he rates 7/10. Which of the following best explains why the TAP block is failing to control this component of his pain?

choices.text: The local anesthetic volume was insufficient to reach the T6-T9 dermatomes supplying the upper abdomen
choices.rationale: A volume problem would manifest as inadequate somatic coverage of part of the abdominal wall, but this patient's incisional (wall) pain is well controlled, so dermatomal spread was adequate.

choices.text: The block was placed too late and the local anesthetic had not yet taken effect
choices.rationale: His abdominal wall analgesia is already established, demonstrating the block has taken effect, so onset timing does not account for the persistent deep cramping pain.

choices.text: Phrenic nerve involvement from cephalad spread has referred pain to the abdomen
choices.rationale: The TAP plane does not communicate with the phrenic nerve, and phrenic block would cause respiratory symptoms rather than visceral abdominal cramping.

choices.text: The TAP block covers only somatic afferents of the abdominal wall and does not block visceral nociception
choices.rationale: Correct: the TAP block anesthetizes the anterior rami (T6-L1) supplying the abdominal wall, providing somatic analgesia only, so visceral pain from the bowel and peritoneum traveling via sympathetic/splanchnic afferents is unaffected.

choices.text: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity has paradoxically lowered his pain threshold
choices.rationale: LAST produces neurologic and cardiac toxicity, not a selective increase in visceral pain, and there is no described systemic toxicity here.

explanation: The TAP block deposits local anesthetic in the fascial plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, blocking the thoracolumbar anterior rami (approximately T6-L1) that supply the anterolateral abdominal wall. Because these are somatic nerves, the block reliably treats incisional and abdominal wall pain but does NOT cover visceral nociception from the bowel and peritoneum, which is carried by sympathetic afferents traveling with the splanchnic nerves. This is the key limitation of the TAP block and the reason it must be combined with other modalities (systemic opioids, neuraxial analgesia, or other adjuncts) for intraperitoneal surgery; a working block with persistent deep visceral pain is expected, not a sign of block failure.
subtopic: Abdominal wall (TAP) block - somatic vs. visceral analgesia and indications
reference_title: Transversus Abdominis Plane Block (StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf)

verdict.reason: Clinical content is correct and unambiguous. The keyed answer (index 3) is the single best answer: the TAP block deposits LA in the plane between internal oblique and transversus abdominis, blocking the T6-L1 thoracolumbar anterior rami (somatic afferents of the anterolateral abdominal wall) but NOT visceral nociception, which travels via sympathetic/splanchnic afferents. Persistent deep cramping visceral pain with a well-functioning block (controlled incisional pain) is the classic, expected limitation after intraperitoneal surgery like sigmoid colectomy. All four distractors are genuinely inferior and correctly defeated by their rationales: insufficient volume (index 0) is excluded because wall pain is controlled; delayed onset (index 1) is excluded because the block is clearly working; phrenic referred pain (index 2) is physiologically wrong and would cause respiratory not visceral symptoms; and LAST selectively lowering pain threshold (index 4) is a fabricated mechanism with no signs present. The explanation correctly notes neuraxial analgesia/opioids as needed adjuncts for visceral coverage. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547730/ resolved to the StatPearls 'Transversus Abdominis Plane Block' article, which is precisely on-topic and directly corroborates the key claim, stating 'A major limitation of the TAP block is that while it provides somatic analgesia, it fails to provide analgesia for visceral pain' and confirming T6-L1 coverage. Both clinical and reference checks pass.

stem: A 72-year-old woman is scheduled for total knee arthroplasty, and the team plans a single-shot spinal anesthetic plus a continuous epidural catheter for postoperative analgesia. She has atrial fibrillation and was bridged with therapeutic (treatment-dose) enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously every 12 hours after her warfarin was held; her INR today is 1.1 and platelet count is 240,000/microL. Her most recent therapeutic enoxaparin dose was administered at 8:00 PM the previous evening. Following current ASRA guidelines on neuraxial procedures in the anticoagulated patient, what is the most appropriate plan for proceeding with the neuraxial technique?

choices.text: Proceed now, since 12 hours have elapsed since the last enoxaparin dose
choices.rationale: Twelve hours is the interval required after a prophylactic (low-dose) LMWH regimen, not after therapeutic dosing, so proceeding now would expose her to an unacceptable spinal hematoma risk.

choices.text: Proceed now after confirming a normal anti-Xa level, regardless of dose timing
choices.rationale: ASRA does not endorse routine anti-Xa monitoring to time neuraxial procedures because the assay does not reliably predict bleeding risk, so it cannot substitute for the recommended time interval.

choices.text: Wait until at least 24 hours have elapsed since the last therapeutic enoxaparin dose before placing the block
choices.rationale: Correct: ASRA guidelines specify a minimum 24-hour interval after the last dose of therapeutic (treatment-dose) LMWH before needle placement to allow adequate drug clearance and minimize epidural hematoma risk.

choices.text: Proceed with the spinal but avoid the epidural catheter, since a catheter cannot be used with any LMWH
choices.rationale: Epidural catheters can be managed with LMWH using appropriate timing of insertion, dosing, and removal, so the catheter is not categorically contraindicated; the governing issue here is the time since the last therapeutic dose.

choices.text: Administer protamine to reverse the enoxaparin, then proceed immediately
choices.rationale: Protamine only partially reverses LMWH anti-Xa activity and is not recommended by ASRA as a means to shorten the waiting interval before a neuraxial procedure.

explanation: ASRA guidelines distinguish prophylactic from therapeutic LMWH dosing when timing neuraxial procedures: needle/catheter placement should occur at least 12 hours after a prophylactic dose but at least 24 hours after a therapeutic (treatment) dose such as enoxaparin 1 mg/kg every 12 hours. This patient received a therapeutic dose at 8:00 PM, so only waiting the full 24 hours adequately reduces the risk of catastrophic spinal/epidural hematoma. Anti-Xa monitoring is not recommended to guide timing, and protamine provides only incomplete reversal, so the time-based interval remains the cornerstone of safe practice.
subtopic: Neuraxial anesthesia in the anticoagulated patient - ASRA LMWH timing
reference_title: Regional Anesthesia in Patients Receiving Antithrombotic or Thrombolytic Therapy: Part 1 (OpenAnesthesia)

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. Per current ASRA guidelines (4th ed., 2018), therapeutic/treatment-dose LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg q12h is the canonical example) requires at least 24 hours after the last dose before neuraxial needle/catheter placement (grade 1C), versus 12 hours for prophylactic dosing. The keyed answer (index 2, the 24-hour wait) is the single best answer. The stem is internally consistent: therapeutic dosing, INR 1.1 (warfarin effect resolved), platelets 240k (no thrombocytopenia confounder), last dose 8:00 PM so only ~12h would have elapsed by a typical morning case — confirming that proceeding now is unsafe. All distractors are genuinely inferior with accurate rationales: A correctly attributes 12h to prophylactic dosing; B correctly notes ASRA does not endorse routine anti-Xa monitoring to time procedures (assay does not reliably predict bleeding risk); D correctly states catheters are not categorically contraindicated with LMWH but require appropriate timing; E correctly states protamine only partially/incompletely reverses LMWH anti-Xa activity and is not endorsed to shorten the interval. Explanation is accurate and current. REFERENCE: The reference_url (OpenAnesthesia 'Regional Anesthesia in Patients Receiving Antithrombotic or Thrombolytic Therapy: Part 1') resolves successfully and is directly on-topic, explicitly stating 'Needle placement should occur at least 12 hours after LMWH dose' (prophylactic) and 'at least 24 hours after LMWH dose' (therapeutic). Citation is valid and relevant. No issues found on either axis.

stem: A 68-year-old man undergoes off-pump coronary artery bypass grafting. After successful grafting, the surgeon asks for heparin reversal. The CAA administers protamine, beginning a 30 mg slow IV dose. Within 90 seconds, the systemic arterial pressure falls from 118/72 to 58/40 mmHg, the central venous pressure rises from 8 to 22 mmHg, the pulmonary artery pressure climbs from 30/14 to 70/40 mmHg, and TEE shows an acutely dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle with leftward bowing of the interventricular septum. Which intervention most directly addresses the underlying pathophysiology of this reaction?

choices.text: Administer inhaled nitric oxide and an inhaled prostacyclin (e.g., epoprostenol)
choices.rationale: Correct: this is a catastrophic (Type III) protamine reaction with acute pulmonary vasoconstriction and RV failure, and selective pulmonary vasodilators directly reduce RV afterload while sparing systemic pressure.

choices.text: Give a 1-liter bolus of crystalloid to raise preload in the failing right ventricle
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the RV is already acutely dilated and overdistended with a CVP of 22 mmHg, so further volume worsens septal shift and impairs LV filling.

choices.text: Administer a beta-blocker such as esmolol to control heart rate and reduce myocardial oxygen demand
choices.rationale: Incorrect: negative inotropy and chronotropy further compromise an already failing, afterloaded right ventricle and lower cardiac output.

choices.text: Re-heparinize to full dose and emergently re-initiate cardiopulmonary bypass as the first maneuver
choices.rationale: Incorrect: while CPB or VA-ECMO is a salvage option in refractory cases, it does not treat the pulmonary vasoconstriction itself and is not the first-line targeted intervention.

choices.text: Administer intravenous sodium nitroprusside to lower the elevated pulmonary pressures
choices.rationale: Incorrect: a nonselective IV vasodilator drops already-critical systemic pressure and coronary perfusion to the RV without selectively unloading the pulmonary circuit.

explanation: Catastrophic protamine reactions (often classified Type III) involve acute thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction, causing a sudden rise in pulmonary artery and central venous pressures, acute RV dilation, leftward septal shift, and systemic hypotension from impaired LV filling. The targeted treatment is to reduce RV afterload with selective pulmonary vasodilators—inhaled nitric oxide and/or inhaled prostacyclin—which lower pulmonary pressures without dropping systemic vascular resistance. Volume loading and nonselective IV vasodilators worsen the picture, and mechanical support (CPB/VA-ECMO) is reserved for refractory collapse.
subtopic: Protamine reactions and right ventricular failure after cardiopulmonary bypass

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem describes a classic catastrophic protamine reaction with acute thromboxane-mediated pulmonary vasoconstriction and acute RV failure (dilated/hypokinetic RV, leftward septal bowing, PA 70/40, CVP 22, systemic hypotension from impaired LV filling). The keyed answer (inhaled NO + inhaled prostacyclin) most directly targets the pathophysiology by selectively reducing RV afterload while sparing systemic SVR. All distractors are genuinely inferior: crystalloid bolus worsens an already overdistended RV (CVP 22) and septal shift; esmolol adds negative inotropy/chronotropy to a failing afterloaded RV; IV nitroprusside is nonselective and drops critical systemic/coronary perfusion; re-heparinization/CPB is a salvage maneuver, not first-line targeted therapy, and does not treat the vasoconstriction. The Horrow/Type III classification, the thromboxane A2 mechanism, and selective pulmonary vasodilator management are all accurate and current. REFERENCE: WebFetch of https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK547753/ resolves to the StatPearls 'Protamine' article (Applefield/Krishnan, updated 2023), which explicitly describes catastrophic pulmonary hypertension from thromboxane A2 release with elevated PVR and prostacyclin infusion as treatment. The citation is on-topic and directly supports the keyed answer.

stem: A 54-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer presents with dyspnea, hypotension (BP 88/64 mmHg), heart rate 122/min, jugular venous distension, and pulsus paradoxus of 16 mmHg. Echocardiography shows a large circumferential pericardial effusion with right atrial and right ventricular diastolic collapse. She is taken urgently for pericardial drainage. Which combination of management choices is most appropriate during induction and airway management?

choices.text: Maintain spontaneous ventilation and prep/drape the patient awake before induction, using ketamine to preserve sympathetic tone
choices.rationale: Correct: keeping the patient spontaneously breathing and avoiding the preload reduction of positive-pressure ventilation until the surgeon is ready to drain best preserves cardiac output in tamponade, and ketamine supports heart rate, contractility, and SVR.

choices.text: Perform a rapid-sequence induction with propofol and immediate positive-pressure ventilation to secure the airway quickly
choices.rationale: Incorrect: propofol blunts sympathetic tone and lowers SVR while positive-pressure ventilation reduces venous return, a combination that can precipitate cardiovascular collapse in a preload-dependent patient.

choices.text: Induce with a high-dose volatile anesthetic via mask to achieve a deep plane before instrumentation
choices.rationale: Incorrect: volatile agents produce dose-dependent myocardial depression and vasodilation, undermining the compensatory tachycardia and contractility needed to maintain output.

choices.text: Aggressively lower the heart rate with esmolol to improve diastolic filling time before induction
choices.rationale: Incorrect: the compensatory tachycardia maintains cardiac output when stroke volume is fixed, and slowing the rate reduces output and can be fatal.

choices.text: Apply high levels of PEEP after induction to recruit lung and optimize oxygenation before drainage
choices.rationale: Incorrect: PEEP raises intrathoracic pressure and further impedes venous return to an already underfilled right heart.

explanation: The hemodynamic goals in pericardial tamponade are summarized as 'fast, full, and tight'—maintain a higher heart rate and contractility, preserve preload, and keep SVR elevated. Positive-pressure ventilation and PEEP raise intrathoracic pressure and reduce venous return, so spontaneous ventilation should be preserved and the patient prepped and draped awake so drainage can occur immediately after induction. Ketamine is favored because it supports heart rate, contractility, and systemic vascular resistance while preserving spontaneous breathing, whereas propofol and volatile agents cause vasodilation and myocardial depression.
subtopic: Pericardial tamponade: induction physiology and ventilation strategy

verdict.reason: CLINICAL: Sound and unambiguous. The stem describes textbook tamponade (metastatic breast CA causing malignant effusion, hypotension/tachycardia/JVD, pulsus paradoxus 16 mmHg >10, echo RA + RV diastolic collapse). Keyed answer A correctly bundles the three cardinal management principles: preserve spontaneous ventilation, prep/drape awake and induce only when the surgeon is ready to drain ("induce on the table"), and ketamine to preserve HR/contractility/SVR/spontaneous breathing — matching the "fast, full, and tight" goals.
